The glorious personage Ezekiel saw in vision seemed to take hold of him, and he was carried in spirit to Jerusalem. There, in the inner court of the temple, a place was prepared for a worthless idol. The whole scene was presented in vision to the prophet. If it should please God to give any man a clear view of his glory and majesty, and of all the abominations happening in any one city, he would then admit the justice of the most severe punishments God should inflict on that city.

A secret place was opened, where the prophet saw creatures painted on the walls, and a number of Israel’s elders worshipped before them. No superiority in worldly matters will preserve people from lust or idolatry when they are left to their own deceitful hearts; and those who are quickly weary in God's service often begrudge no effort nor expense when following their superstitions. When hypocrites hide behind the appearance of outward devotion, there is some flaw or other left uncovered, something that betrays them to those who look carefully. There is a great deal of secret wickedness in the world. They think themselves out of God’s sight. But those are ripe for ruin who lay the blame for their sins upon the Lord.

The yearly lamenting for Tammuz was accompanied by shameful practices; and the worshippers of the sun described here were likely priests. The Lord appeals to the prophet concerning the wickedness of the crime, "and look, they put the branch to their nose," indicating some custom used by idolaters in honor of the idols they served. The more we examine human nature and our own hearts, the more abominations we will discover; and the longer a believer searches himself, the more he will humble himself before God, and the more he will value the fountain open for sin, and seek to wash within it.
